query: published cases of cerebromediastinal tuberculosis associated with Say-Barber-Miller syndrome in children

retrieved_docs.title: Horner Syndrome -- Evaluation -- Labs
retrieved_docs.content: The initial workup should include a complete blood count (CBC), erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), and serum chemistry panel. Urine or blood cultures may be ordered if an infectious agent is suspected. Testing for suspected neurosyphilis, HIV, thyroid function, vitamin B-12, and folate levels may be ordered if indicated following detailed history and examination. Urine testing for elevated metabolic catecholamine by-products is important in the pediatric population with suspected neuroblastoma. Purified protein derivative (PPD) is warranted in suspected tuberculosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Tuberculosis in Children -- History and Physical -- Central nervous system disease
retrieved_docs.content: The treatment of tubercular meningitis requires a regimen of prolonged antituberculosis therapy, initially with 4 drugs. (Please refer to the Treatment section for more information on antituberculosis regimens). Management of increased intracranial pressure may require additional medications, including steroids, mannitol, and acetazolamide. The placement of a temporary or permanent ventriculoperitoneal shunt may be needed. [42] [41] Close outpatient follow-up is required to manage adherence and potential adverse events from non TB medications, particularly electrolyte disturbances with the use of acetazolamide.

retrieved_docs.title: Tuberculosis in Children -- History and Physical -- Central nervous system disease
retrieved_docs.content: The most deadly and debilitating form of TB is meningitis, which disproportionately affects young children. Without antibiotic therapy, corticosteroids, and critical care management of fluids, electrolytes, and elevated intracranial pressure, tuberculous meningitis is rapidly fatal within several weeks. [35] Despite advances in the care of children with tubercular meningitis, mortality is still high; more than 50% of survivors have neurodevelopmental disabilities. [35] [36] [37] [38] Areas of the world with a high burden of TB may be more likely to recognize the disease than others but lack some tools for fully successful diagnosis and treatment. In contrast, high-resource areas lack knowledge and experience with the disease to recognize signs and symptoms early on. [38]

retrieved_docs.title: CNS Tuberculosis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The different CNS-TB forms produce various manifestations. The historical and physical features of each type are explained below.

retrieved_docs.title: Tuberculosis in Children -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Children with TB infection are a small but often overlooked population and represent a repository for future cases if not treated to prevent reactivation disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Tuberculosis in Children -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Key facts to keep in mind for TB in children:

retrieved_docs.title: Tuberculous Meningitis -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Despite being a preventable and curable disease, tuberculosis is the leading cause of death worldwide due to an infectious etiology; approximately one-third of the world’s population is presumed to be infected with MTB. TBM carried a fatal prognosis before the development of antituberculosis medications, and it remains the number 1 cause of death and disability in children infected with MTB. TBM may also occur during immune reconstitution syndrome that can occur shortly after treatment initiation for HIV with antiretrovirals when undiagnosed MTB infection is present.

retrieved_docs.title: Tuberculosis in Children -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Unfortunately, the differential diagnosis of TB is highly dependent upon the location of the disease. As noted in the clinical description of TB, many more common childhood diseases may be mistakenly diagnosed in a child with TB. Other viral, bacterial, and fungal types of pneumonia can present similarly, and M tuberculosis should always be considered in these cases. Cervical lymphadenitis can be viral, especially if multiple nodes are involved. Bacterial pathogens, eg, Bartonella and atypical mycobacteria, can also cause cervical, axillary, and inguinal lymphadenitis; atypical mycobacteria, like M tuberculosis , may produce purplish-red skin discoloration during the progression of the infection. M tuberculosis meningitis is often missed until late as it can mimic other more common childhood viral and bacterial infections that affect the central nervous system.

retrieved_docs.title: Miliary Tuberculosis -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Central nervous system involvement, particularly TBM, can occur and presents a serious and life-threatening complication.

retrieved_docs.title: CNS Tuberculosis -- Evaluation -- Clinical Diagnosis of Tuberculous Meningitis
retrieved_docs.content: Identifying active extraneural infection occasionally aids in determining a presumptive diagnosis. Chest radiographs are beneficial in most pediatric TBM cases and approximately 50% of adult cases (see Image . Pulmonary Tuberculosis). Laboratory analysis may indicate mild anemia, lymphocytosis, and hyponatremia. A positive tuberculin test offers supportive evidence, notably in children, though a negative result does not rule out the diagnosis. Funduscopy may also uncover papilledema and choroid tubercles, aiding diagnosis (see Image . Tuberculoma on Funduscopy). [28]

retrieved_docs.title: CNS Tuberculosis -- History and Physical -- Tuberculous Meningitis
retrieved_docs.content: Children may have prominent irritability, vomiting, and focal or generalized convulsions. Individuals with untreated TBM may not survive beyond 5 to 8 weeks of illness. CN palsies are seen in 20% to 30% of patients with TBM, mostly involving CN VI. [18] OCA with blindness is a significant TBM complication. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Tuberculosis in Children -- History and Physical -- Central nervous system disease
retrieved_docs.content: Urgent evaluation of the central nervous system with head imaging is crucial, followed by lumbar puncture for cultures and fluid analysis. [42] Brain imaging commonly shows basilar meningeal enhancement, hydrocephalus, enhancement of basal ganglion, infarcts, or a combination of these. [43] [44] Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is preferred over computed tomography for its improved ability to identify early infarcts, basal ganglia enhancement, and exudates in the basal cisterns. [45] [46] [47] However, MRI is not widely available in low-resource countries (see Image. Tuberculosis Meningitis).

retrieved_docs.title: Tuberculosis in Children -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: The presentation of TB in children is varied and can involve all organ systems. In low-burden countries, a high index of suspicion is required to reach the proper diagnosis, as M tuberculosis disease can masquerade as many common childhood illnesses. A child with TB always represents a secondary case; the index case adult must be located and treated.

retrieved_docs.title: Kluver-Bucy Syndrome -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Herpes simplex encephalitis (HSE) [9] Stroke (temporal lobe infarction - usually bilateral) [10] [11] Listeria meningoencephalitis [12] Traumatic brain injury [13] Central nervous system tuberculosis [2] Primary cerebral Whipple disease [14] Alzheimer disease [15] Pick disease [16] Hypoglycemia [17] Acute sporadic porphyria [18] Huntington disease [19] Juvenile neuronal lipofuscinosis [20] Toxoplasmosis Epilepsy [21] Parkinson disease Heat stroke [22] Shigellosis [23] Methamphetamine withdrawal [24] Systemic lupus erythematosus [25] Anoxic-ischemic encephalopathy [26] Neurocysticercosis [26] Non-Hodgkin lymphoma [27] Mycoplasmal bronchitis. [28] Methotrexate leukoencephalopathy [29] Subdural hygroma [30] Susac syndrome [31] - associated with partial KBS Anti-NMDAR encephalitis [32] Exposure to cannabis [33] The most common pathologies leading to the development of KBS are head injury and stroke in adults and herpes simplex encephalitis in children.

retrieved_docs.title: Tuberculosis in Children -- History and Physical -- Central nervous system disease
retrieved_docs.content: The clinical onset of tuberculous meningitis can be acute or gradual in the early stages. Nonspecific symptoms, such as flu-like illness, low-grade fever, malaise, and pulmonary symptoms, may overshadow central nervous system findings. Many children are initially seen with pneumonia that will not resolve, poor appetite, and vomiting before more serious signs of central nervous system disease become manifest. Advanced disease may demonstrate signs of meningeal irritation (eg, elevated intracranial pressure with a bulging fontanelle, sunsetting sign, and papilledema), cranial nerve palsies, neurologic deficits and loss of milestones, altered sensorium, and seizures. [39] [40] [41] The Glasgow Coma Scale can help assess the child's consciousness level and aid in determining the ultimate prognosis. [35]

retrieved_docs.title: CNS Tuberculosis -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: CNS-TB's prognosis is dismal even after therapy. TBM's mortality ranges between 9.8% and 57%, as it is associated with distressing levels of neurologic sequelae that disproportionately afflict children. [109] Various mortality and neurological morbidity predictors have been identified, including age, symptom duration, Glasgow Coma Scale score, absence of headache, CSF protein level, arachnoiditis extent, presence of complications, and Medical Research Council staging at presentation. [110]

Neonatal pneumonia, a child of the neonatal lung infection category, is clinically associated with a positive tuberculin test, suggesting a link between pulmonary infection and latent Mycobacterium tuberculosis exposure. Fetal malnutrition (not specified as small for gestational age) and intracerebral bleeding in the fetus or newborn are placed within disorders of infants and newborns. RDS serves as a parent condition for general infant‑newborn disorder, respiratory disorder, and lung disorder, is a member of the standardized newborn disorder SMQ, and is linked to an unspecified perinatal condition and the immature lung syndrome, highlighting its central role in perinatal respiratory pathology. Fetal growth restriction shares the same unspecified perinatal condition, which is also the parent of unspecified perinatal infections included in the neonatal disorder SMQ. Extreme prematurity with unspecified weight is another SMQ member. The

This child modality provides detailed cross‑sectional images of the thoracic cavity, facilitating evaluation of pulmonary and mediastinal conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 3 -- Community 2184
retrieved_docs.content: The term “hrudník,” referring to the chest, denotes the anatomical location where pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse can develop. These three thoracic conditions are also linked with “دبيلة,” indicating an association between the clinical manifestations of pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse and the entity دبيلة. Understanding that the chest (hrudník) is the site of these pathologies and recognizing their connection to دبيلة is important for accurate diagnosis and effective management of patients presenting with pleural and pulmonary disorders.

Both general pulmonary inflammation and bacterial pneumonia are frequently associated with a positive tuberculin skin test (فحص التوبركولين إيجابي), a finding similarly observed in skull enlargement (حج القحف), suggesting overlapping immunologic or infectious markers. Bacterial pneumonia possesses a transmissible component (přenos), underscoring the need for infection‑control measures when managing affected patients.

Consequently, babies classified as خديج are at risk for respiratory complications arising from lung immaturity, highlighting the importance of recognizing this gestational age category when assessing and managing neonatal respiratory health.

Abnormal cytological, histological, immunological and DNA test findings are recorded under immunizations and screening for infectious disease; a positive tuberculin test is clinically associated with neonatal pneumonia, abnormal head‑circumference measurements and abnormal chromosome‑analysis results. Birth trauma is connected to intracerebral bleeding in the fetus, representing a serious neurologic consequence of traumatic delivery. Metabolic syndrome is identified as a comorbidity that may coexist with these neonatal conditions. Overall, the hierarchy connects gestational age and growth parameters to a spectrum of neonatal pathologies (respiratory distress, infections, metabolic and traumatic disorders) and to maternal complications (diabetes, hypertension), providing clinicians with a structured framework for categorizing, diagnosing and managing perinatal and early‑life health conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 1089
retrieved_docs.content: with or lead to infectious complications around birth. Overall, the knowledge network portrays neonatal disorders as an interconnected group of conditions that include traumatic cerebral injury, intracranial and neonatal hemorrhage, perinatal infections, metabolic diseases such as transient neonatal diabetes, neurologic problems like developmental delay and epilepsy, and extreme prematurity. Birth trauma is a key antecedent of intracranial bleeding, which is classified under neonatal hemorrhage, while perinatal infection serves both as a parent condition for unspecified perinatal disorders and as a member of the broader neonatal disorder set. Recognizing these relationships aids clinicians in comprehensive assessment, diagnosis, and management of newborns presenting with any of these interrelated pathologies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1826
retrieved_docs.content: The category “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions” is presented as a parent concept that includes the specific child entity “غشي.” In this framework, the general term “Symptoms” is described as closely related to, and potentially synonymous with, the broader phrase “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions,” indicating that the two expressions refer to overlapping clinical manifestations. Consequently, the specific manifestation “غشي” is understood to fall under the umbrella of nonspecific or ill‑defined symptomatology, and clinicians should recognize its inclusion within this broader symptom category when evaluating and documenting patient presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 1483
retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory distress syndrome is identified across languages, with the Czech term syndrom dechové tísně and the Arabic term اِضطِراب الرِّئَة listed as its parent entries, and it is cross‑referenced to the Arabic designation متلازمة خلل نضج الرئة. These synonymous labels all describe the same clinical condition—a neonatal lung disorder caused by insufficient surfactant that leads to severe breathing difficulty—thereby supporting consistent recognition and management of the disease in multilingual medical contexts.
